coredata.dc:title: Quantification of neomycin in rubella vaccine by off/on metal ion mediated photoluminescence from functionalized graphene quantum dots

coredata.dc:description: The determination of neomycin sulfate was made using photoluminescent amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (obtained from hydro-exfoliation of a mixture of citric acid and glutathione). From the several ions tested, Fe3+ was the best mediator to enable an off/on photoluminescence effect used for quantification. The mediation of Fe3+ was found to be crucial as it is responsible for the photoluminescence quenching effect, due to the interaction with quantum dots surface, also having large affinity towards neomycin that removes Fe3+ from the surface of GQDs, consequently, promoting restoration of the original nanomaterial photoluminescence. Such signal restoration was proportional to the neomycin sulfate concentration added. The linearized analytical response covered three orders of magnitude (10−7 to 10−5 mol L−1). The proposed method is an alternative to those requiring labor-intensive procedures for chemical the derivatization of neomycin (due to the lack of chromophore groups in aminoglycosides). The method was successfully tested in the analysis of rubella vaccine containing trace residues of neomycin and in pharmaceutical compositions containing neomycin sulfate after solid phase extraction using an aminoglycoside imprinted polymer to improve selectivity in determinations.

X-ray photoelectron spectrometry general survey scans of GQDs-GSH: (A) High resolution C 1 s peaks corresponding to C- sp2 and sp3, C-N (-O), and COOH groups, respectively. (B) High resolution N 1s peaks corresponding to C-N and graphitic C respectively. (C) High resolution for O 1s peaks corresponding to O-C and O-Si in SiO2.

A) Extinction spectra from: (a) GQD-GSH in pure water, (b) Fe3+ in pure water, (c) GQD-GSH with Fe3+ after stabilization time. B) Photoluminescence spectra measured at 345/425 nm from a GQDs-GSH diluted dispersion in the presence of increasing amounts of metal Fe3+: (a) 0; (b) 5.0 × 10−7; (c) 2.5 × 10−6; (d) 5.0 × 10−6; (e) 1.0 × 10−5; (f) 1.5 × 10−5; (g) 2.0 × 10−5; (h) 2.5 × 10−5 mol L−1.

(A) Photoluminescence spectra from a GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ aqueous dispersion (a) before and (b) after the addition of neomycin (2.5 × 10−7 mol L−1); (B) Extinction spectra of form GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ aqueous dispersion (a) before and (b) after addition of neomycin (5.0 × 10−6 mol L−1).

(A) Normalized response curves for the interaction of increasing concentration of neomycin with the GQD-GSH-Fe3+ probe with the final concentration of Fe3+ in solution of: (A) 1.0 × 10 −5, (B) 1.5 × 10 −5, (C) 1.8 × 10 −5 mol L−1. (B) Effect of temperature on the GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ photoluminescence in the presence of different concentrations of neomycin sulfate (from 1.0 × 10−7 to 2.0 × 10−6 mol L−1). Where L0 is the luminescence measured in absence of neomycin and Ln are the photoluminescence intensities measured in the presence of neomycin sulfate.

A) Photoluminescence from the GQDs-amino-Fe3+ probe with increasing concentrations of neomycin: (a) 0 (b) 1.7 × 10−7, (c) 6.8 × 10−7, (d) 1.7 × 10−6, (e) 2.5 × 10−6, (f) 3.3 × 10−6, (g) 4.2 × 10−6, (h) 5.0 × 10−6, (i) 6.7 × 10−6, (j) 8.3 × 10−6 mol L−1. B) Analytical curve based on the GQDs-GSH photoluminescence restoration induced by neomycin (expressed as L-L0/L0 in function of the concentration of neomycin).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The determination of neomycin sulfate was made using photoluminescent amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (obtained from hydro-exfoliation of a mixture of citric acid and glutathione). From the several ions tested, Fe3+ was the best mediator to enable an off/on photoluminescence effect used for quantification. The mediation of Fe3+ was found to be crucial as it is responsible for the photoluminescence quenching effect, due to the interaction with quantum dots surface, also having large affinity towards neomycin that removes Fe3+ from the surface of GQDs, consequently, promoting restoration of the original nanomaterial photoluminescence. Such signal restoration was proportional to the neomycin sulfate concentration added. The linearized analytical response covered three orders of magnitude (10−7 to 10−5 mol L−1). The proposed method is an alternative to those requiring labor-intensive procedures for chemical the derivatization of neomycin (due to the lack of chromophore groups in aminoglycosides). The method was successfully tested in the analysis of rubella vaccine containing trace residues of neomycin and in pharmaceutical compositions containing neomycin sulfate after solid phase extraction using an aminoglycoside imprinted polymer to improve selectivity in determinations.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are graphene flakes in the nanometer scale and since they are under the influence of quantum confinement effect, it becomes a semi-conductor and presents special properties of quantum dots [1–3] that enabled them to be used in a myriad optical applications including photocatalysis [4], photoelectric conversion [5], in optoelectronic devices [6–8], as optical sensors [9–11] and as markers for bio-imaging [12,13].

Photoluminescence of GQDs, when dispersed in a medium (usually aqueous solutions), can be perturbed by the interaction with either electron acceptor or electron donor chemical species through long-term binding or by long distance energy transfer. In this sense, functional modification and assembly of GQDs are important to achieve variations in physical and chemical properties that enable different optical responses [14]. In principle, GQDs contain carboxylic acid groups attached on their edges, enabling further chemical modifications by covalent bonds and by non-covalent interactions with inorganic ions, organic compounds and biomolecules [15]. Interactions may disturb GQDs optical properties affecting luminescence intensity (through quenching or amplification) sometimes promoting spectral shifts and even lifetime changes [16]. The use of metal ions to mediate the spectral changes is one of the strategies to enable GQDs to be used as sensors [17–20]. For instance, quantum dots of europium-doped graphene (Eu-GQDs) were used as an off-on luminescent probe for detection of L-cysteine (L-Cys) in serum samples. The luminescence intensity of Eu-GQDs was quenched in the presence of Cu2+ due to the coordination reaction between Cu2+ and carboxyl groups on the surface of the doped carbon dot. Luminescence was recovered with the subsequent addition of L-cysteine due to the strong affinity to Cu2+, enabling a linear response in the range of 0.5 to 50 mmol L−1 [20]. The photoluminescence quenching of amino-functionalized GQDs caused by histamine, through mediation of Fe3+, Cu2+, Eu3+, was studied and the sensitivity of normalized photoluminescence quenching curves indicated a tenfold stronger interaction of histamine with the Fe3+ coordinated GQDs surface. The linear quenching induced by histamine (measured at 345/435 nm) covered concentration range from 0.43 μmol L−1 to 320 μmol L−1 [21]. Nitrogen-mediated and co-doped sulfur (N,S-GQDs) GQDs was used as luminescent probe for Fe3+, Cu2+ and Ag+ by using masking agents to selectively detect each of these ions at the nmol L−1 level [22]. More recently, N,S-GQDs were obtained by hydro-exfoliation of a pyrolyzed mixture of citric acid and D-penicillamine, leading to efficient coordination with Hg2+ (at the nmol L−1 level) that produced a selective signal quenching) [23].

Neomycin is a widely used aminoglycoside antibiotic that inhibit the growth of several gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria [24]. The uncontrolled use of neomycin causes, in humans, ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity [25,26]. In vaccines, neomycin is used as preservative to increase shelf life. Analytical methods for the determination of neomycin are available based on immunologic assay [27] optical response from gold nanoparticles [26,28], fluorescence after chemical derivatization [29,30], electrochemical processes promoted by modified electrodes [31]. However, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) is the dominant technique to determine neomycin since it allows efficient separation from other chemical species and can be easily coupled to tandem-mass spectrometry [32] to electrochemical detection systems [33,34] or to fluorimetric detectors [35], in this case requiring pre-column or post-column chemical derivatization of neomycin with fluorogenic reagents such as phenylisocyanate and o-phthalaldehyde.

A new trend in detection aminoglycoside antibiotics is the use of optical probes constituted by quantum dots (QDs). Khan et al. developed a method for the determination of kanamycin sulfate based on the enhanced photoluminescence of thioglycolic acid modified CdTe QDs. The analytical curve covered two orders of magnitude and the limit of detection (LOD) was 1.4 × 10−8 mol L−1 [36]. Wan et al. combined glutathione modified CdTe QDs with a neomycin-imprinted polymer for the determination of neomycin. The sensor displayed a linear concentration-dependent luminescence quenching in the analyte concentration range from 3.2 × 10−9 to 1.6 × 10−6 mol L−1 [37]. However, the use of this type of inorganic QDs as photoluminescent probes has been limited due to the toxicity of Cd and instability of the nanomaterial over time. Compared to inorganic QDs, GQDs are superior in terms of stability and biocompatibility, which enabled studies using dispersions of GQDs as analytical probes [38]. Toloza et al. used amino-functionalized GQDs associated with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) for the determination of kanamycin sulfate. AuNPs were produced by the chemical reduction of AuCl4 directly in aqueous dispersion of GQDs (obtained by the pyrolysis of citric acid and glutathione) also containing the cationic surfactant CTAB. The AuNPs-GQDs-amino-CTAB system showed a suppressed photoluminescence which was amplified in the presence of kanamycin, following a linear trend (from 10−7 to 10−5 mol L−1) [39].

Considering that neomycin is an important drug used as conservative in vaccines and because aminoglycosides present poor optical properties in the UV–vis, functionalized GQDs modified with Fe3+, in aqueous dispersions, were used as off/on probes for ultra-trace determination of neomycin under adjusted conditions to achieve robustness and selectivity in analytical response.

Photoluminescence measurements were made on a model LS 55 luminescence spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer) with solutions/dispersions placed in 1 cm optical path length cuvette with four polished quartz windows. A thermostatic system with stirring (PTP-1 Fluorescence Peltier System with a PCB1500Water Peltier System, Perkin-Elmer) was used to keep the aqueous dispersions of nanoparticles in the cuvette at constant temperature. The UV–vis absorption double beam spectrophotometer (Lambda 35) was from Perkin-Elmer (UK) with solutions/dispersions placed in 1 cm optical path length cuvette with two polished quartz windows. Microscopies from nanoparticles were made using a field emission scanning electron microscope (JEOL, model JSM-6701F, Japan) operating in the scanning transmission mode (STEM) at 30 kV. Raman spectroscopic measurements were performed on a micro-Raman spectrometer (NT-MDT, model NTegra spectra, Russia). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy measurements were made on a model Alpha 110 hemispherical electron energy analyzer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with a dual anode electron gun covered with magnesium as photoelectrons source. Zeta potential measurements were made using a nanoparticle analyzer model SZ-100 (Horiba, Japan) using an acrylic electrochemical cell containing a flat carbon electrode (6 mm thickness). Chromatographic analyses were made on a high performance liquid chromatography system (Model 1200, Agilent Technologies, Japan) equipped with a multi wavelength fluorescence detector, a column oven (kept at 30 °C) and a Agilent Eclipse XDB-C18 column (250 × 4.6 mm and 5 μm average particle size). For the pH measurements, it was employed a pH-meter model MPA 210 Tecnopon (Brazil) with a glass membrane electrode combined with a reference Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl).

Ultrapure water (< 18.2 MΩ cm) was obtained from the Milli-Q gradient A10 ultra-purifier (Milipore, USA). Neomicyn sulfate, gentamicyn sulfate, kanamycin sulfate, reduced L-glutathione (GSH), europium (III) nitrate (Eu(NO3)3·5H2O) and nickel nitrate (NiCl2.6H2O) were form Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Iron (III) chloride (FeCl3.6H2O), cadmium chloride (CdCl2.H2O), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2) and copper (II) sulfate (CuSO4.5H2O) were from Vetec (Brasil). Iron (II) sulfate (FeSO4.7H2O) was from J.T. Baker (USA) and zinc sulfate (ZnSO4.7H2O) was from Reagen (Brazil). The (3-aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTMS) (≥ 98%), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) (99.9%), o-phthalaldehyde (OPA) (≥ 99%) and 2-mercaptoethanol (99.0%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Quinine sulfate were purchased from Fluka (Germany). The reagents o-phthalaldehyde (OPA), trichloroacetic acid (TCA), tetrahydrofuran (THF), sodium hydroxide, sodium acetate, hydrochloric acid and citric acid were obtained from Merck (Germany). Triethylamine was from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). HPLC-grade solvents (methanol and acetonitrile) were form Merck. Syringe filters (0.45 μm) were from Whatman, UK. Nitrogen (99.999% purity) was from Lynde gases, Brazil. Dialysis membranes (retained molecular weight of 3.5 kDa) were purchased from Spectrum Laboratories Inc. (USA).

The production of the different GQDs, through hydro-exfoliation of fused reagents, was based on a procedure described in the literature [7] using citric acid or mixture of citric acid and another substance (used as chemical modifier). The chemical modifiers used were: i) glutathione (GSH), ii) thioacetamide (TAA) and iii) thiourea (SN). When only citric acid was used, the mass of substance was about 0.50 g. When mixtures of citric acid and other substances were used, the chemical modifier/citric acid mass proportion used was 0.15 with total mass of about 0.62 g. The solid mixture was placed into a 5 mL beaker to be heated at 240 °C in a heating plate. As the mixture became fluid with the color of fused material becoming brownish (within 2 to 5 min of heating), the hot liquid was verted into 50 mL of room-temperature ultrapure water in order to obtain a clear pale yellow aqueous sample (called original dispersion). The original dispersion was placed in a membrane to be dialyzed for 24 h in order to obtain cleaner GQDs aqueous dispersion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of standard solutions, sample solutions and working GQDs dispersions
ce:section.ce:para: Standard stock solutions of aminoglycosides, at 5.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 and at 5.0 × 10−3 mol L−1, were prepared by dissolving appropriate amounts of their sulfate salts in ultrapure water. Stock solutions from all metal ions, except Eu3+ and Fe3+, were 1 × 10−1 mol L−1 and prepared by dissolution of salts in water. Stock solutions of Eu3+ and Fe3+ were 1 × 10−2 mol L−1. The dialyzed original GQDs dispersion made using citric acid and GSH (GQDs-GSH) was ten-fold diluted (10% v/v) with ultrapure water in order to obtain an intermediary GQDs-GSH dispersion. Then, 240 μL of this intermediary dispersion was placed on a 10.00 mL volumetric flask where appropriate volumes of a metal ion (Mn+) stock solution was added before adjusting final volume with water, achieving a Mn+ concentration of 5.0 × 10−3 mol L−1 in the system where the GQDs-GSH-Mn+ was formed. The working dispersions used for neomycin determination were prepared by placing 9.00 mL of the GQDs-GSH-Mn+ dispersions into a 10.00 mL volumetric flask. Then appropriate volumes of neomycin sulfate solution were added before adjusting the final volume with water. Blank dispersions had their final volume adjusted only with water.

For HPLC analyses, the neomycin sulfate aqueous stock solution (1000 mg L−1) was used to prepare, by sequential dilution, the analyte standards (from 25 to 250 μg mL−1). The derivatization solution was prepared by dissolving 0.053 g of OPA in 2.0 mL of methanol and then adding 2-mercaptoethanol (40 μL) and diluting to 10 mL with borate buffer solution (0.4 mol L−1; pH 9.5). This solution was protected from light and it was used the same day it was prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Raman and scanning transmission electron microscopy measurements
ce:section.ce:para: About 25 μL (5 drops) of aqueous dispersions of GQDs-GSH were deposited on a silicon substrate and left to dry at room-temperature and atmospheric pressure. Then, Raman measurements were made by probing sample with a 473 nm laser source. Samples for the scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) measurements have not been submitted to any specific treatments. They were analyzed by placing a few microliters of the colloidal dispersions on carbon grids and allowed to dry at room temperature for further analysis by STEM.

Photoluminescence spectra were made using 1000 nm min−1 scan rate and 10.0 nm spectral bandpass. Quantitative measurements were made in steady state at fixed wavelength pair. Appropriate volumes of aminoglycosides standard solution were added to 10.00 mL volumetric flasks before the working dispersion of GQDs-GSH or GQDs- GSH-Mn+ had their final volumes adjusted with water. After manual mixing, these dispersions were transferred to quartz cuvettes to measure photoluminescence at the following excitation/emission wavelength pairs: GQDs-GSH (345/425 nm); GQDs- GSH-Fe3+ (345/435 nm); GQDs-GSH-Cu2+ (345/428 nm) and GQDs-GSH-Eu3+ (345/428 nm). Blank measurements were made in aqueous dispersions of the nanomaterials (GQDs-GSH or GQDs-GSH-Mn+) without addition of the aminoglycosides. Net photoluminescence measured from dispersions containing aminoglycosides (L−L0) were normalized by their respective blank signals (L0) in order to establish an increasing relationship between normalized signal, (L-L0)/L0, and neomycin sulfate concentration.

Sample solutions of pharmaceutical compositions and vaccine containing neomycin sulfate were prepared by successive dilution of the original sample in order to adjust the expected analyte concentration to be determined by the proposed method. The topical ophthalmic pharmaceutical solution containing neomycin sulfate (8.2 × 10−3 mol L−1 or 5.83 mg mL−1) also contained lidocaine sulfate, fluocinolone acetonide and polymixin B sulfate. The rubella vaccine sample supposedly might have traces of neomycin sulfate in its composition, with the major components being human albumin, lactose, sorbitol, mannitol, in concentrations not specified by manufacturer. The vaccine was fortified with neomycin sulfate (2.5 × 10−4 mol L−1 or 0.17 mg mL−1) in order to be studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of an imprinted polymer using kanamycin as a template
ce:section.ce:para: The aminoglycoside selective molecularly imprinted polymer (AMG-MIP) was synthesized based on the procedure described by Almeida et al. [40]. Kanamycin sulfate was chosen as a template rather than neomycin because it was more stable at room-temperature, very soluble in water and it was the cheapest aminoglycoside available. About 500 mg of kanamycin sulfate was dissolved in 6 mL of deionized water, where it was added: 3200 μL of APTMS, 2650 μL of TEOS and 400 μL of 1 mol L−1 aqueous HCl solution. The mixture was heated to 40 °C under controlled stirring. The obtained gel was cooled to room-temperature and left standing for 48 h, to ensure dryness of the material. The polymer was crushed and washed with 1 L of deionized water and 300 mL of methanol to remove the kanamycin sulfate. This cleaning process was repeated for two more times. Finally, the polymer was sieved to obtain regular size distribution of particles with diameters between 106 and 150 μm. The polymer was analyzed by scanning electron microscopy - energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDX) revealing the following composition: 31% of silicon, 44.3% of oxygen and 24.8% of carbon (Fig. S1).

The aminoglycoside selective molecularly imprinted polymer (AMG-MIP) was synthesized based on the procedure described by Almeida et al. [40]. Kanamycin sulfate was chosen as a template rather than neomycin because it was more stable at room-temperature, very soluble in water and it was the cheapest aminoglycoside available. About 500 mg of kanamycin sulfate was dissolved in 6 mL of deionized water, where it was added: 3200 μL of APTMS, 2650 μL of TEOS and 400 μL of 1 mol L−1 aqueous HCl solution. The mixture was heated to 40 °C under controlled stirring. The obtained gel was cooled to room-temperature and left standing for 48 h, to ensure dryness of the material. The polymer was crushed and washed with 1 L of deionized water and 300 mL of methanol to remove the kanamycin sulfate. This cleaning process was repeated for two more times. Finally, the polymer was sieved to obtain regular size distribution of particles with diameters between 106 and 150 μm. The polymer was analyzed by scanning electron microscopy - energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDX) revealing the following composition: 31% of silicon, 44.3% of oxygen and 24.8% of carbon (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solid phase extraction of neomycin sulfate
ce:section.ce:para: In order to enhance selectivity towards the neomycin sulfate, solid-phase extraction, with a cartridge packed with a laboratory-made AMG-MIP was used. The cartridge was packed with 100 mg of the polymer. The sample solution was loaded into the stoppered cartridge, leaving the analyte to interact with solid-phase during 1 h. Clean up elution was made with aliquots (1 mL) of water in order to remove excipients. The aminoglycoside, retained on the cartridge, was eluted with 0.5 mL of acidic aqueous solution (HCl 0.01 mol L−1). The pH of the eluted aliquot containing the neomycin was adjusted to around pH 4 before addition into the analytical probe dispersions (GQD-GSH-Fe3+ at pH 4). In order to force complete elution of the retained analyte into a single aliquot, an acidic aqueous solution (pH 3.0 adjusted by the addition of 0.01 mol L−1 of HCl) was used. After elution of analyte, the pH of the aliquot was adjusted to 4.0 (by the addition of 0.01 mol L−1 NaOH) before being mixed with the GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ dispersion for photoluminescence measurements. In summary, after elution with five aliquots of water (pH 7), the forced elution of the analyte occurred at the 6th elution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chromatographic method for the determination of neomycine
ce:section.ce:para: The HPLC method used to determine neomycin was based on the chemical derivatization of neomycin using o-phthalaldehyde (OPA). The separation was performed using isocratic elution and mobile phase containing 66% (in volume) of an organic solvent mixture (86.7% of acetonitrile and 13.3% methanol) and 34% of phosphate buffer solution (10 mmol L−1, pH 3.5) [31]. The stationary phase was a Zorbax eclipse C18 column (250 × 4.6 mm and a 5 μm average particle size). The mobile phase flow rate was 1.0 mL min−1 and the introduced volume into the HPLC system was 50 μL at room temperature. The neomycin derivated was monitored by fluorescence with excitation wavelength at 345 nm and emission wavelength at 450 nm. Prior to the injection into the HPLC system, an aliquot of 400 μL of sample or standard was mixed with 150 μL of derivatization solution and left for reaction for 10 min.

The production of GQDs was made using different precursors (including mixtures of compounds) aiming to achieve different degrees of functionalization of GQDs (in edges and/or in surface defects). Therefore, it was expected to achieve a diversity in photoluminescence characteristics, stability of GQDs in aqueous solution (due to the creation of different densities of surfaces charges) also differences in sites for the interaction with the target analyte. Four different precursors were pyrolyzed and hydro-exfoliated to produce GQDs. The first was citric acid and the other three were mixtures of citric acid and another substance (chemical modifier) as indicated: i) citric acid/glutathione (GSH), ii) citric acid/thioacetamide (TAA) and iii) citric acid/thiourea (SN). When mixtures of substances were used, the chemical modifier/citric acid mass proportion used was 0.15 with total mass of about 0.62 g.

After dialysis, an appropriate aliquot (in the range of 10 to 1000 μL) of the different original dispersions of GQDs were selected to be diluted in water (10.00 mL final volume) in order to achieve luminescence within the instrument detection range prior to detector saturation. Spectral characteristics, in terms of maximum wavelength of excitation and emission, were very similar but relative photoluminescence, taking into account the measured signal and the dilution factor used to prepare the measured nanomaterial dispersion, was significantly higher (from 588 to 1000 times) for the GQDs prepared in the presence of GSH (GQDs-GSH) when compared to others (Table 1 ). The following test was made by adjusting quantities of nanomaterials (by selecting proper volumes of the dialyzed dispersion) to prepare diluted dispersions (diluted in water) that were fortified with specific quantities of different aminoglycosides (neomycin, gentamicin, kanamycin, streptomycin) and erythromycin (a macrolide antibiotic) with final concentration of antibiotic within the range from 1.3 × 10−7 and 2.3 × 10−5 mol L−1. In all cases, the presence of antibiotics caused no effect on the photoluminescence of the different GQDs.

As reported in literature, GQDs dispersed in water (pH in the range of 3.8 to 5) present negative surface charge enabling them to interact with cations [2]. For GQDs-GSH, it was obtained a zeta-potential (ζ-potential) of (51.7 ± 4.3) mV. Therefore, photoluminescence from GQDs-GSH could be affected by organic analytes (of some ligand capacity) through mediation of cations previously attached to the surface of nanomaterials [2,6]. In general, attached cations withdraw electrons from the GQDs valence band, inhibiting exciton recombination, thus decreasing the observed photoluminescence. If the relative binding force of the organic analyte towards the metal ion is higher than the one of the functionalized nanomaterial, the removing of the cation from the surface of the nanomaterial lead to photoluminescence restoration, enabling an off/on effect that could be useful in the analytical point of view [7].

Several metals ions (Cd2+, Cu2+, Eu3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ni2+ and Zn2+) were tested as potential mediators to enable aminoglycosides to affect photoluminescence from carbon quantum dots. Aliquots of stock solutions of these cations were added to the different synthesized GQDs dispersions (previously diluted in water). The final concentrations of cations were fixed at 5.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 and photoluminescence was measured after 60 min equilibration time. In such conditions, the observed effects were classified in three types: I) insignificant photoluminescence quenching (none or <5% signal decreasing); II) moderate quenching (from 5 to 15% signal decreasing); III) effective quenching (>15% signal decreasing). Type I effect occurred in the presence of Zn2+ and Cd2+ for all of the different types of GQDs. Moderate quenching (type II) was produced by Ni2+ only in the case GQDs-TAA, being ineffective for the other types of GQDs. Similarly, Fe2+ produce type II effect in the case of GQDs-GSH and type I effect in the other cases. Eu3+ produced type II effect when added to dispersions of GQDs (the one prepared only from citric acid), GQDs-GSH and GQDs-SN, being insignificant the effect observed in dispersions containing GQDs-TAA. The cations Cu2+ and Fe3+ were more effective photoluminescence quenchers as Cu2+ produced about 30% and 25% signal decreasing (type III effect) when added respectively to dispersions of GQDs-GSH and GQDs-SN. Moderate effect was observed in the presence of GQDs and GQDs-TAA. In the case of Fe3+, type III effect was observed in the presence of GQDs-GSH (28%), GQDs-TAA (18%) and GQDs-SN (32%) and type II effect in the case of GQDs. The summarized results are presented in Table 2 .

To the aqueous dispersions fortified with cations, neomycin was added (at final concentration of 5.0 × 10−7 mol L−1) in order to evaluate if there were some degree of photoluminescence restoration (in cases when cation produced previous quenching). Results are also summarized in Table 2 and the only case in which significant photoluminescence restoration occurred was found for GQDs-GSH in the presence of Fe3+. The signal restoration achieved by adding neomycin was about 65% of the original GQDs-GSH signal magnitude. In addition, replicate experiments indicated a reproducible photoluminescence off/on effect in this case. Similar results were achieved with the other aminoglycosides and with erythromycin. Based on these preliminary tests, the system comprising of GQDs-GSH as the luminophore and Fe3+ as a photoluminescence off/on mediator was chosen for the determination of neomycin,

The good interaction between GQDs-GSH and Fe3+ indicated that the mixing of GSH in the fused and hydro-exfoliated material was somehow effective in functionalizing the carbon nanomaterial (GQDs). GSH molecular structure comprises amino, carboxylic acid and thiol groups. In fact, indications of amino functionalization of GQDs, prepared using GSH, have been reported in the literature [7].

In order to obtain quantitative information about the synthesized nanomaterials, a total carbon (TC) analysis was made using an aliquot of the GQDs-GSH after dialysis (24 h). The TC measured was 131.6 mg L−1 (value already corrected by the TC value obtained from water placed inside a dialysis membrane). After 100-fold dilution of the dialyzed original dispersion, the resultant GQDs-GSH aqueous dispersion (diluted dispersion) presented an extinction spectrum (Fig. 1A) with an intense maximum (below 220 nm) and a shoulder (at 345 nm, corresponding to the first excitonic). Excitation at 345 nm produced a bright blue-violet photoluminescence (emission band) with maximum at 435 nm (Fig. 1B). Neomycin (at 5.0 × 10−3 mol L−1) does not absorb at 345 nm and presents no photoluminescence when excited at 345 nm. Those results indicate that suppression of photoluminescence by inner filter effect would not be expected in mixtures containing GQDs-GSH and neomycin.

The quantum yield of the GQD-GSH was 49.5%, in water at 25 °C, and the clear appearance and strong photoluminescence was stable even after 11 months, when stored around 2 and 5 °C. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements of the GQDs-GSH dispersed in water indicated hydrodynamic average diameter of 22 ± 9 nm with size range from 10 to 40 nm (Fig. 2A). Microscopic images by FE-STEM (Fig. 2B), after processing using Image-J software, provided average diameters for the carbon nanoparticles of 19 ± 8 nm, which corroborates the value obtained by DLS if considering the solvation shell around the nanoparticles computed in the hydrodynamic diameter. Microscopy also indicated a deviation of circularity of GQDs-GSH (average circularity of 0.7 ± 0.1). A certain degree of clustering on nanoparticles could also be observed. Raman peaks characteristic of graphene were found (Fig. 2C) near 1579 cm−1, assigned to the G band (characteristic of E2g phonon of sp2 carbon atoms) and near 1360 cm−1 (assigned to the D band associated with defects in graphene).

X-ray photoelectron spectrometry was also used to probe the dispersion of GQDs-GSH. The general spectra presented peaks that are characteristic for carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. In a closer spectra evaluation (Fig. 3A), C 1 s peaks were identified at 284.6, 285.8 and 287.8 eV, which were assigned to the contributions of CC sp2, sp3 CO or/and (CN), and CO, respectively. Spectra also showed N 1 s main peak (Fig. 3B) with binding energy at 399.5 eV, which corroborates that the nitrogen eventually attached to the surface of the nanomaterial is mainly organic such as the ones typical of amines (CN). The second peak, at 401.0 eV, corresponds to graphitic bound N. In (Fig. 3C), O 1 s peaks were identified at 530.6 and 532.5 eV, which were assigned respectively to OC and OSi (probably from SiO2 due to the substrate used for XPA analysis). The XPS spectral features strongly indicated the functionalization of GQDs with nitrogen, which affected significantly photoluminescence, since the relative signal obtained for the GQDs-GSH tend to be three orders of magnitude more intense than the one obtained for GQDs (citric acid only). Analysis performed of nanoparticles using SEM-EDX (Fig. S1) indicated carbon (51.9%) in addition to nitrogen (2.9%) and sulfur (2.6%).

X-ray photoelectron spectrometry was also used to probe the dispersion of GQDs-GSH. The general spectra presented peaks that are characteristic for carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. In a closer spectra evaluation (Fig. 3A), C 1 s peaks were identified at 284.6, 285.8 and 287.8 eV, which were assigned to the contributions of CC sp2, sp3 CO or/and (CN), and CO, respectively. Spectra also showed N 1 s main peak (Fig. 3B) with binding energy at 399.5 eV, which corroborates that the nitrogen eventually attached to the surface of the nanomaterial is mainly organic such as the ones typical of amines (CN). The second peak, at 401.0 eV, corresponds to graphitic bound N. In (Fig. 3C), O 1 s peaks were identified at 530.6 and 532.5 eV, which were assigned respectively to OC and OSi (probably from SiO2 due to the substrate used for XPA analysis). The XPS spectral features strongly indicated the functionalization of GQDs with nitrogen, which affected significantly photoluminescence, since the relative signal obtained for the GQDs-GSH tend to be three orders of magnitude more intense than the one obtained for GQDs (citric acid only). Analysis performed of nanoparticles using SEM-EDX (Fig. S1) indicated carbon (51.9%) in addition to nitrogen (2.9%) and sulfur (2.6%).

As photoluminescence of GQDs-GSH tend to be quenched in the presence of Fe3+, a further investigation of this phenomenon was made. First, extinction spectra (Fig. 4A) from GQDs-GSH diluted dispersions (at their characteristic pH about 4.0 ± 0.2) have shown a different spectral feature (with red-shifting) when mixed with Fe3+ (see spectrum of a standard Fe3+ solution for comparison). Such a spectral change indicates transition of electrons, under photo-excitation, between GQDs-GSH and Fe3+, typical of a charge-transfer complex, in this case formed by coordination of the nanoparticles, through functional groups, with cations.

By increasing the added quantities of Fe3+ (final concentrations in the range from 10−7 to 10−4 mol L−1) into the GQDs-GSH diluted dispersion, a continuous photoluminescence quenching was observed until a limit was achieved at Fe3+ concentration of 2.5 × 10−5 mol L−1 (Fig. 4B). The presence of higher quantities of Fe3+ (tested up to 4 × 10−4 mol L−1) caused no further signal quenching in the GQDs-GSH system. In such conditions, the maximum decreasing of signal was about 85%, when compared to the original photoluminescence of the diluted nanomaterial dispersion in absence of Fe3+. No relevant spectral shitting in photoluminescence spectra were observed as the concentration of Fe3+ was added. The presence of Fe3+ also caused a drastic change the nature of the surface charge of the nanoparticles (ζ-potential of +50.6 ± 2.5 mV).

In order to evaluate the time required to promote effective photoluminescence quenching, the signal from the GQDs-GSH diluted dispersion was monitored, in the quartz cuvette, right after addition and mixing of an aliquot of Fe3+ stock solution (final concentration 1.5 × 10−5 mol L−1) and every 5 min up to 120 min, at room temperature. The dispersion fortified with Fe3+ was not any longer disturbed after the initial stirring. It was found that photoluminescence quenching sharply decreases during the first 10 min of the experiment with signal stabilizing after 20 min, remaining constant up to 120 min (random variation of signal and standard deviation of <1%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the interaction of aminoglycosides with GQDs-GSH-Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of aminoglycosides, for instance by the addition of neomycin, sulfate at final concentration of 2.5 × 10−7 mol L−1, to the dispersion of GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ tended to restore the original photoluminescence of the GQDs-GSH with no significant shifting of luminescence spectrum maxima (Fig. 5A). In addition, the presence of neomycin produced a change in the extinction spectra of the nanoparticles dispersion (GQDs-GSH-Fe3+) with a signal decreasing and a slightly spectral blue-shifting (Fig. 5B). Such a behavior indicated that neomycin produces a perturbation in the charge-transfer complex formed by the interaction between GQDs-GSH and Fe3+. Zeta potential measurements strongly indicated that aminoglycosides remove the Fe3+ bound to the surface of the nanoparticles, as ζ-potential positive values of GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ decreased as the concentration of aminoglycosides increased (Table 3 ).

As photoluminescence recovery was achieved by the addition of aminoglycosides as sulfates, a further experiment was made to evaluate if the anion sulfate was actually the one responsible for signal recovery rather than the aminoglycoside. Therefore, sodium sulfate (at final concentrations in orders of magnitude varying from 10−7 to 10−5 mol L−1) was added to the system and no perturbation was observed in the photoluminescence measured from the GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ dispersion.

Aminoglycoside efficiency in restoring the original GQDs-GSH photoluminescence was dependent upon the amount of Fe3+ previously added to the system. As Fe3+ were added in excess, the photoluminescence restoring became less efficient, which indicate that free cation is preferred in complexing with aminoglycoside rather than the Fe3+ bound to the nanoparticle. This is illustrated by the sensibility of the curves in (Fig. 6A), showing that as Fe3+ is increased the signal restoration capability of neomycin decreases. The best compromise between initial quenching magnitude by Fe3+ and sensitivity in response towards neomycin was found using probes containing 1.5 × 10−5 mol L−1 final concentration of Fe3+.

The effect of the pH on the photoluminescence restoring effect, caused by the addition of neomycin, was evaluated in the range from 2.0 to 5.5, since higher pH values would affect the stability of Fe3+ in solution. Random signal variation (about 5%) along the tested acidic range was observed indicating, in principle, no influence on the interaction between neomycin and GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ even in system adjusted to pH values above 4 from where some degree of hydrolysis of Fe3+ would be expected. This result is another evidence that Fe3+ binds and it is stabilized on the surface of GQDs-GSH.

Zeta potential of the GQDs-GSH dispersions containing Fe3+ was measured at pH 3.0 + (49.3 ± 3.5 mV) and at pH 5.0 + (52.7 ± 4.3 mV). In addition, increasing concentrations of neomycin (1.0 × 10−7 and 1.0 × 10−6 mol L−1) were added to these dispersions, prepared at pH 3.0 and at pH 5.0, and no difference was found in the capability to recover the GQDs-GSH photoluminescence. Therefore, since the original pH of the probes (between 4.0 ± 0.2 pH units) was within the tested range, no pH adjustment was made when using them to detect neomycin.

The interaction between aminoglycoside and the GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ was evaluated by monitoring photoluminescence in function of time. It was found that signal recovery induced by the addition of neomycin sulfate (at final concentration of 5.0 × 10−6 mol L−1) took about 20 min to reach maximum value and stable conditions (evaluated up to 120 min). It is also relevant to mention that these experiments have shown that the order of addition of components and time are important and must be the following: addition of Fe3+ stock solution into the GQDs-GSH aqueous dispersion and, after 30 min, the addition of neomycin sulfate followed by the adjustment of volume with water. Signal measurements should be made after 20 min. As a proof-of-concept, a series of dispersions were prepared by mixing GQDs-GSH, neomycin sulfate (at final concentration range between 1.6 × 10−7 to 8.6 × 10−5 mol L−1) and Fe3+ altogether. Even after 40 min, the signal measured from these dispersions could not be directly related to the concentration of neomycin, showing that Fe3+ and neomycin have readily interacted with no participation of GQDs-GSH. The measured signal was also monitored 12 h after the addition of neomycin into three replicates of the analytical probe (GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ dispersion). No significant changes were observed and signal difference was 2.2 ± 0.3%, which indicated that analytical measurements using the probe were stable for at least 12 h.

The effect of temperature in the photoluminescence response induced by neomycin was also studied in order to better understand the nature of the interaction between the components of the system (GQDs-GSH, Fe3+ and neomycin). Temperature of the GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ dispersions was varied from 20 °C to 40 °C and photoluminescence restoration curve, induced by the addition of neomycin (from 1.0 × 10−7 to 2.0 × 10−6 mol L−1), were constructed (Fig. 6B). It was observed that sensitivity of the photoluminescence restoration curve decreases as the temperature increases, which is a clue that the binding between Fe3+ and neomycin is being affected due to thermal agitation. It is also important to mention that the quenching effect imposed by Fe3+ also is affected (less effective) as temperature increases. These results prove that photoluminescence quenching caused by Fe3+ is not a collisional type and rely on a more permanent interaction that result in withdrawing of electrons from the excited GQDs to the electron-deficient Fe3+. A further experiment was made to evaluate the reversibility of the analytical response in function of the temperature. First, the signal produced by the addition of neomycin sulfate was measured at 20 °C. Then the temperature was increased to 40 °C, which produced about 20% decreasing in signal as the interaction between neomycin with nanoparticles are affected. However, as the temperature was reduced back to 20 °C, the photoluminescence was not only restored but increased to about 50% (56 ± 4%; n = 3) of the original one measured in the presence of neomycin. These results may indicate that temperature cycle somehow may be affecting surface of the GQD-GSH-Fe3+ nanoparticles creating more sites that enabled contact with neomycin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical response of the GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ aqueous probe towards neomycin, kanamycin and gentamycin
Analytical characteristics in determining neomycin by the GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ aqueous probe
ce:section.ce:para: Analytical figures of merit were obtained for neomycin as this aminoglycoside is used to preserve rubella vaccine. The linear response (R2 = 0.994) for neomycin sulfate covered two orders of magnitude up to 8.3 × 10−6 (final concentration of the neomycin in the probe). The analytical curve equation was (L-L0)/L0 = 1.1 × 105 [Neomycin] + 0.3, where L and L0 are the photoluminescence values measured after and before the addition of neomycin. The photoluminescence restoration curve (normalized photoluminescence) induced by neomycin is shown in (Fig. 8 ) along the respective set of spectra.

The limit of detection (LOD) was 7.4 × 10−8 mol L−1, calculated 3s
b
/m, where s
b was the standard deviations of the blank (n = 10) and m is the sensitivity of the curve. The limit of quantification (1.2 × 10−7 mol L−1) was calculated using 10s
b
/m.

Precision of the method (intermediated precision) was evaluated with measurements (at 345/435 nm) from two sets of ten independents probes (each set containing one different concentration of the neomycin). Precision was calculated as the combined standard deviation s (L-L0/L), where sL and sL0 are the standard deviations of L and L0 respectively. For the neomycin, at 1.6 × 10−6 mol L−1, the intermediate precision was 1.6%, whereas for the neomycin at 8.3 × 10−6 mol L−1 it was 1.5%. The instrumental precision was evaluated by ten measurements of a single probe dispersion at two fixed concentrations (1.6 × 10−6 mol L−1 and 8.3 × 10−6 mol L−1) and they were 0.9% and 1.3%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity studies and solid phase extraction with a cartridge packed with a molecularly imprinted polymer
ce:section.ce:para: Interferences imposed by other substances in the sample matrix can only be eliminated by the separation of neomycin sulfate from the samples, which can be efficiently made by solid-phase extraction (SPE). The efficiency of the kanamycin-MIP as the solid-phase to enable selective extraction of aminoglycosides has been made by comparing with the retaining efficiency of the non-printed polymer (NIP). The results, obtained in a previous work [40], showed excellent retaining capability for gentamicin (95.8%), kanamycin (97.3%), neomycin (91.2%) and streptomycin (91.5%), all of them as sulfate. Such retention efficiency is due to the structure similarity of the tested substances with the one of the template (kanamycin). Retention achieved using NIP was below 10%, showing that the kanamycin-MIP is a selective substrate for neomycin. The maximum quantity of analyte retained by a cartridge, packed with 70 mg of kanamycin-MIP, was 3.2 μmol [40].

Selectivity of response was evaluated by testing the effect of a series of substances commonly found in pharmaceutical formulations containing neomycin. Other substances found in vaccine (against measles, mumps and rubella) were also tested. The evaluated substances were individually added into the GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ probe at a relative high concentration (final concentration of 5 × 10−4 mol L−1) when compared to the range for the detection of neomycin. The results obtained showed that sorbitol, lactose, mannitol, polymixin B sulfate, and fluocinolone acetonide produced no significant perturbation in measured signal (photoluminescence variation of <1%). In contrast, human albumin, lidocaine sulfate and EDTA disodium salt caused an amplification of the measured photoluminescence (varying from 15% to 25%).

In order to enhance selectivity towards neomycin sulfate, solid-phase extraction with a cartridge packed with kanamycin-MIP was used. Despite the fact that commercial MIP cartridges are available, it was decided to use an aminoglycoside selective MIP produced through a sol-gel synthesis (using kanamycin sulfate as a template) as reported in literature [36]. In this way, it is intended to retain neomycin in order perform a clean-up to remove substances responsible for interferences before eluting the analyte from the cartridge prior to determination.

Tests were made with SPE cartridge prepared to allow the elution to be around 0.5 mL min−1 by adjusting the packing and the size of the wool stoppering placed at the tip of the cartridge. Three authentic replicas of aliquots of solutions containing neomycin sulfate (17 nmol of neomycin), were loaded into SPE cartridges. After 1 h, to allow the equilibrium between analyte and solid phase to be stablished, a series of aliquots of 1 mL of a mobile phase (comprised of aqueous solution with pH adjusted to 7) were percolated through the cartridges and collected for analysis. After each collection, the pH of the eluted solution was adjusted to the original pH of the probe (pH 4.0 by the addition of 0.01 mol L−1 HCl) before transferred to a volumetric flask containing the GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ probe (to be measured after volume adjusted to 10.00 mL).

When comparing the effect of the original neomycin content on the probe signal with the effect caused by the amount of neomycin recovered after five sequential eluted aliquots, the difference in signal (equivalent to the loss of analyte) was about 8% (n = 3), with more analyte being removed in the fifth elution. Such a loss can be attributed to the fraction of analyte, weakly adjusted to the imprinted cavities of the solid phase, that was removed during the elution with aqueous solution at pH 7. From the sixth to the ninth elution about 92% (n = 3) of analyte was recovered.

In order to force the elution of the retained analyte in one single aliquot, an acidic aqueous solution (pH 3.0 adjusted by the addition of 0.01 mol L−1 HCl) was used to elute analyte after clean up. In this way, after elution, the pH of the aliquot was adjusted to 4.0 before being mixed with the GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ in probe conditions for photoluminescence measurements. In this way, after elution with five aliquots of water (pH 7), the forced analyte elution at 6th elution represented 92.1 ± 0.4% of analyte recovery.

In order to evaluate the MIP efficiency towards neomycin sulfate in the presence of a mixture of interferents, a simulated sample containing 5.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 of neomycin sulfate, as well as, polymixin B, EDTA and lidocaine (those three at 5.0 × 10−2 mol L−1 each, simulating a pharmaceutical composition) or containing albumin, lactose and sorbitol (at 1.7 × 10−4 mol L−1 each one, simulating a vaccine composition). The simulated samples were submitted to the MIP extraction, with fractions having their pH adjusted to 4.0. Excipients were removed mainly during the two first fractions, leaving room to perform cleaning up of samples before elution neomycin sulfate. The forced elution of neomycin sulfate was made at the 6th aliquot, and the recovery obtained for the simulated pharmaceutical composition was of 95.5 ± 3.9%, while for the simulated vaccine composition, the recovery was of 96 ± 2.5%. Despite the fact that a small fraction of the measured signal) may be due to excipients (based on the 92% average recovery of neomycin in the previous test), these results still guarantee an efficient separation using MIP-loaded SPE cartridges in such a complex samples.

A real pharmaceutical composition (neomycin sulfate concentration of 8.2 × 10−3 mol L−1 or 5.8 mg mL−1) was analyzed. The sample was diluted with water in order to obtain a photoluminescence signal for neomycin sulfate proportional for the concentration of 1.5 × 10−6 mol L−1. The 6th collected aliquot in the SPE procedure, obtained using forced elution conditions, was added to the GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ probe and the signal measured indicated a recovery of 95.9 ± 3.7.

As residues of neomycin sulfate can be found at trace level in rubella vaccine, a sample (obtained from a vaccination center in a public hospital) was also analyzed before and after fortification with the analyte (at 2.5 × 10−4 mol L−1). After SPE clean up, the 6th aliquot (obtained after forced analyte elution) was analyzed. The results, found in Table 5 , indicated percent recoveries were above 95.5% in terms of the expected analyte value. Standard deviation (n = 3) was <4%. For the original rubella vaccine sample, no detectable residual neomycin was found.

In order to compare, the same samples were also analyzed by HPLC with molecular absorption detection of the pre-column derivatized neomycin using OPA. The chromatogram obtained from a standard neomycin sulfate solution (derivatized with OPA) shown a symmetric sharp peak (0.981) at the retention time of 9.0 min. Neomycin sulfate recovered results (three replicates) in pharmaceutical samples (real and simulated) were close to the expected values (Table 5).

When comparing results achieved by HPLC with the ones obtained using the proposed GQDs-GSH-Fe3+ probe, the statistical test (two-tailed t-student test) indicated no significant difference. Experimental results for the pharmaceutical formulation (three obtained by HPLC and three obtained by the proposed method) were also compared using analysis of variance (single-factor ANOVA with 95% confidence level and n1 = n2 = 3). These two sets of samples were similar as Fexperimental < Fcritical (Fexperimental = 3.89 and Fcritical = 5.98).

In this work, different metal ions were evaluated as mediators in the interaction of carbon nanoparticles and aminoglycosides. Effective photoluminescence quenching of GQDs-GSH was achieved in the presence of the Fe3+ with the original signal being further recovered as neomycin were added in the aqueous system containing the dispersed nanoparticles, leading to an off/on type optical probe. It was found strong indications that a long term chemical interaction was established between GQDs-GSH and Fe3+ (formation of a charge transfer complex along with photoluminescence quenching) before the aminoglycoside disrupt such a coordination. After stablishing proper experimental conditions and study the nature of optical response, this probe (GQDs-GSH-Fe3+) was evaluated in terms of sensibility and selectivity towards neomycin sulfate. Selectivity was achieved in the analysis of rubella vaccine using solid phase extraction with aminoglycoside selective imprinted polymer. The proposed method is simple and can be used as an alternative to the ones based on costly techniques and to the ones based on the use of toxic chemical derivatization agents to promote measurable optical properties from aminoglycosides.
